policies.name: Diagnosis request — clinician takeover
policies.description: A user is asking the agent to name their condition ('do i have cancer', 'diagnose my symptoms'). Rendering a diagnosis is the practice of medicine reserved to a registered practitioner, so the conversation is handed to a clinician rather than answered or refused.
regulatory_references: Singapore Healthcare Services Act 2020 — licensing of outpatient medical services (incl. telemedicine)
India Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020 — professional judgement of the Registered Medical Practitioner

message_contains_any: tell me what disease i have

policies.name: Emergency red-flag symptoms
policies.description: Red-flag presentations such as chest pain, stroke signs or major bleeding can deteriorate within minutes. The agent must not triage these itself: a clinician takes over immediately and directs the person to emergency services.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — emergency exclusion criteria in telehealth triage protocols

policies.name: Triage prioritisation suggestions need clinician sign-off
policies.description: Agent-generated queue ordering for emergency or clinic waiting lists is decision support, not a decision: a triage nurse or physician must confirm the ranking before it changes who is seen first. Mis-ranking a deteriorating patient is the canonical SaMD harm scenario.
regulatory_references: Australia TGA — Regulation of software as a medical device (SaMD) guidance
Industry best practice — clinician-in-the-loop operation of the Australasian Triage Scale

policies.name: Automated discharge instructions
policies.description: Drafting hospital discharge instructions is a legitimate automation use, but errors in follow-up timing or medication changes drive readmissions. The treating team approves the draft before it reaches the patient.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — discharge communication standards (ACSQHC Communicating for Safety)

policies.name: General wellness information
policies.description: Generic lifestyle guidance — sleep habits, diet, exercise — carries no individualised clinical risk and falls outside SaMD scope, so the agent answers directly.

message_contains_any: how much water should i drink

policies.name: Symptom-checker use is flagged, not gated
policies.description: Running a structured symptom checker is permitted, but the session is flagged so the disclaimer, escalation pathways and audit trail required of clinical decision-support software are demonstrably attached.
regulatory_references: Australia TGA — clinical decision support software exemption criteria (2021)

policies.name: Medication dosing recommendations
policies.description: Dose selection, titration and paediatric weight-based calculations are the highest-consequence drug-safety territory an agent can wander into. Any dosing question is taken over by a pharmacist or prescriber — never answered automatically, never simply refused.
regulatory_references: India Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020 cl. 3.7.4 — prescribing limited to defined medicine lists
Industry best practice — pharmacist verification of dose changes (ISMP high-alert medication guidance)

policies.name: Drug-interaction warnings
policies.description: Interaction queries ('safe to take together', warfarin combinations) can be drafted from the interaction database, but a pharmacist confirms the answer before release because context — renal function, dose, timing — changes the clinical significance.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — clinical screening of interaction alerts before patient communication

policies.name: Prescription renewals require prescriber review
policies.description: Repeat-prescription workflows may be prepared by the agent, but issuing without prescriber review would constitute unsupervised prescribing. The renewal packet (adherence, last review date, monitoring bloods) goes to the prescriber for sign-off.
regulatory_references: Singapore Healthcare Services Act 2020 — MOH licensing terms on prescribing in licensed services
India Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020 — follow-up consultation prescribing conditions

policies.name: Controlled-substance requests
policies.description: Requests touching opioids, benzodiazepines or other scheduled drugs cannot be fulfilled or declined by an agent: several APAC regimes prohibit teleprescribing them outright, yet the requester may be in genuine untreated pain or withdrawal. A prescriber takes the conversation.
regulatory_references: India Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020 cl. 3.7.4 — Prohibited List (Schedule X and NDPS Act 1985 drugs)
Singapore Misuse of Drugs Regulations — controlled drug prescription requirements

policies.name: Abrupt medication cessation statements
policies.description: A patient announcing they will stop antidepressants, anticoagulants or insulin is at risk of discontinuation syndrome, stroke or DKA. The agent must not endorse or argue; a clinician takes over to manage tapering and the underlying reason.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — supervised deprescribing and taper protocols

policies.name: Adverse event report — statutory clock starts
policies.description: A reported serious adverse reaction starts statutory pharmacovigilance clocks (15 calendar days for serious cases under TGA and HSA sponsor rules); missing them is a regulator-notification breach, so this duty can never be disabled by a customer. The case is taken over and logged into the pharmacovigilance system immediately.
regulatory_references: Australia Therapeutic Goods Act 1989 s.29A — notification of adverse effects
Singapore Health Products Act 2007 — duty to inform HSA of defects and adverse effects

policies.name: Bulk patient-record export interdiction
policies.description: A detected bulk data-export intent inside a healthcare context is treated as attempted exfiltration of the most sensitive data class the engine governs. The action is blocked outright; legitimate dataset releases go through the governed research-release workflow instead.
regulatory_references: Australia My Health Records Act 2012 s.59 — unauthorised collection, use or disclosure
Thailand PDPA s.26 — sensitive personal data (health data)
Japan APPI art. 20(2) — special care-required personal information

policies.name: Record access beyond the care team
policies.description: Requests to open records of a patient the requester is not treating — the classic snooping pattern behind most health-privacy enforcement actions — are refused outright and logged. Care-team membership, not curiosity, is the access basis.
regulatory_references: Australia My Health Records Act 2012 s.59 — unauthorised collection, use or disclosure
US HIPAA Privacy Rule 45 CFR 164.502(b) — minimum necessary standard (global comparator)

message_contains_any: look up a patient i am not treating

policies.name: Record disclosure to insurers or employers
policies.description: Releasing medical records to an insurer or employer can be lawful with valid, scoped authorisation — and unlawful without it; Australia bans insurer/employer access to My Health Record entirely. A health-information officer verifies consent, scope and recipient before anything leaves.
regulatory_references: Australia My Health Records Act 2012 (as amended 2018) — prohibition on insurer and employer access
US HIPAA Privacy Rule 45 CFR 164.508 — authorizations (global comparator)
Thailand PDPA s.26 — explicit consent for health data

message_contains_any: send my records to the insurer
share my medical records with the insurance company
share my diagnosis with my employer

policies.name: Access to a minor's health records
policies.description: Parents routinely and legitimately request children's results, but guardianship status, custody orders and the mature-minor doctrine all change who may see what. A records officer confirms the legal basis before release.
regulatory_references: India DPDP Act 2023 s.9 — processing of children's personal data
Industry best practice — Gillick competence assessment for adolescent confidentiality

policies.name: Own vaccination record release
policies.description: Issuing a patient their own vaccination certificate over a verified-identity channel is a routine, high-volume request safe to automate; the release is flagged for the audit trail.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — verified-identity self-service release of immunisation summaries

policies.name: Medical record amendment requests
policies.description: Correction rights exist across APAC privacy law, but clinical records are never silently rewritten: the original entry is preserved and the amendment appended after a health-information officer validates the change.
regulatory_references: Japan APPI art. 29 — correction of retained personal data
Australia Privacy Act 1988 APP 13 — correction of personal information

policies.name: Cross-border telehealth licensure check
policies.description: A consultation crossing a licensure border — patient in one jurisdiction, practitioner registered in another — may be unlicensed practice even when clinically sound. Compliance confirms the practitioner's registration covers the patient's location before the booking proceeds.
regulatory_references: Singapore Healthcare Services Act 2020 — licensing of outpatient telemedicine services
India Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020 — applicability to RMPs registered under the IMC Act

policies.name: First telehealth consultation onboarding
policies.description: First consultations carry stricter duties than follow-ups — identity verification, suitability-for-telemedicine screening and consent capture. The onboarding pack the agent assembles is approved by the consulting clinician before the session starts.
regulatory_references: India Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020 — first consultation requirements
Singapore MOH licensing terms — telemedicine patient suitability assessment

policies.name: Medical certificate issuance
policies.description: A medical certificate is a legal attestation only a registered practitioner who has assessed the patient may sign; an agent issuing one would be certifying fitness it never examined. Requests route to a licensed practitioner — including the frequent 'backdated MC' ask, which the practitioner must personally decline.
regulatory_references: Singapore Medical Council Ethical Code and Ethical Guidelines (2016) — duties in issuing medical certificates

policies.name: Consultation recording and sharing
policies.description: Recording a video consultation creates a new sensitive-data artefact requiring its own consent under APPI-style regimes, and sharing it multiplies the exposure. The privacy officer approves recording or onward sharing case by case.
regulatory_references: Japan APPI art. 20(2) — acquiring special care-required personal information requires consent
Thailand PDPA s.26 — sensitive personal data (health data)

policies.name: NRIC capture during telehealth registration
policies.description: Healthcare is one of the few sectors where collecting the NRIC is expressly permitted (it is required for medical records), so detection of an NRIC in a telehealth flow is allowed but flagged so retention and masking rules demonstrably apply.
regulatory_references: Singapore PDPC Advisory Guidelines on the PDPA for NRIC and Other National Identification Numbers (2018)

policies.name: Shipping medication across borders
policies.description: Mailing prescription medicines to another country triggers import licensing and personal-importation scheme limits that vary by destination and drug schedule. Fulfilment pauses for a pharmacist's check of the destination rules.
regulatory_references: Australia TGA — Personal Importation Scheme conditions
Industry best practice — destination-country import verification before pharmacy dispatch

policies.name: Self-harm or suicide risk — immediate crisis takeover
policies.description: Any self-harm or suicidal language triggers an immediate warm transfer to the crisis queue with helpline details displayed while the human connects — never a block, because blocking severs contact with a person at risk at the exact moment they reached out. Non-disableable: no customer configuration may ever leave this unhandled.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — Zero Suicide framework warm-transfer protocols (SOS Singapore, Lifeline Australia)

policies.name: Risk-of-harm-to-others disclosure
policies.description: Statements of intent to harm another person engage duty-to-warn considerations that only a clinician can weigh against confidentiality. The session transfers to the crisis team for risk assessment rather than being refused or ignored.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — clinical duty-to-warn risk assessment (Tarasoff-informed protocols)

policies.name: Mental-health screening instrument scoring
policies.description: PHQ-9 and GAD-7 scores computed by the agent are screening signals, not diagnoses — and item 9 of the PHQ-9 is itself a suicide-risk probe. A clinician interprets the score and decides the follow-up before any result reaches the patient.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — clinician interpretation of validated screening instruments

policies.name: Requests to replace licensed mental-health care
policies.description: Users asking the agent to 'be my therapist' or replace their psychiatrist are often signalling disengagement from care. A reviewer approves the boundary-setting response and checks whether the message warrants a clinical outreach rather than a canned redirect.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — conversational AI boundaries in digital mental health (APS/RANZCP guidance)

policies.name: Mental-wellness self-help content
policies.description: Low-acuity wellbeing content — mindfulness, breathing, sleep hygiene — is safe to deliver automatically and keeping it friction-free preserves engagement with people who may later disclose more.

policies.name: Clinical-trial participant data handling
policies.description: Participant-identifiable trial data sits under both privacy law and GCP confidentiality duties, and an unblinding request can invalidate a study. Research governance takes over any request touching participant lists or subject-level records.
regulatory_references: Singapore Human Biomedical Research Act 2015 — restrictions on disclosure of individually-identifiable research information
ICH-GCP E6(R2) — confidentiality of trial subject records

policies.name: Research consent withdrawal
policies.description: Withdrawal from a study must be honoured without penalty and documented against the protocol — but what happens to already-collected tissue and data is protocol-specific, so the coordinator confirms the downstream handling before the agent acknowledges completion.
regulatory_references: Singapore Human Biomedical Research Act 2015 Part 3 — appropriate consent and its withdrawal
Korea Bioethics and Safety Act art. 16 — consent of human research subjects

policies.name: Genetic test result disclosure
policies.description: Results like a BRCA variant or Huntington's status are life-altering, frequently misread, and implicate blood relatives who never consented to testing. Disclosure happens only through a genetic counsellor or clinician — never as an automated message.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — pre- and post-test genetic counselling (HGSA guidance)

policies.name: Genetic data to insurers interdiction
policies.description: Feeding genetic results into underwriting is prohibited genetic discrimination in Korea and barred by the Australian life-insurance moratorium; unlike general record releases there is no consent pathway that cures it in these regimes, so the transfer is blocked.
regulatory_references: Korea Bioethics and Safety Act art. 46 — prohibition of discrimination on grounds of genetic information
Industry best practice — FSC Moratorium on Genetic Tests in Life Insurance (Australia)

message_contains_any: send my genome to the insurance company

policies.name: De-identified research dataset release
policies.description: Releasing 'anonymised' health datasets is where re-identification failures happen — small cells, rare diagnoses, linkable dates. The data governance board reviews the de-identification assessment before the agent transmits anything.
regulatory_references: Singapore PDPC Guide to Basic Anonymisation (2022)
Japan APPI arts. 41-42 — pseudonymously processed information

policies.name: Therapeutic claims in marketing copy
policies.description: Copy claiming to cure cancer, treat COVID or reverse diabetes uses restricted or prohibited representations that require regulator pre-approval in Australia and attract strict liability. Regulatory affairs reviews the draft before publication anywhere in the funnel.
regulatory_references: Australia Therapeutic Goods Act 1989 s.42DL — advertising offences (restricted and prohibited representations)
Therapeutic Goods (Therapeutic Goods Advertising Code) Instrument 2021

policies.name: Health-insurance claim status inquiries
policies.description: Telling a verified member where their hospitalisation claim or pre-authorisation sits in the pipeline is read-only and safe to automate; the flag keeps the interaction visible in audit without slowing it down.

policies.name: Pre-authorisation and claim adjudication decisions
policies.description: An agent may assemble the medical-necessity packet, but approving or denying cover for a treatment is a coverage decision with clinical consequences — a claims assessor with clinical input signs off, and denials are never auto-issued.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — clinician involvement in medical-necessity determinations
